blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.5

For fixed \(u_1 = u : H \to G\), the functor \((\mathbf{Sch})^{\circ}_{/S} \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) which associates with every T over S the set of homomorphisms of T-groups \(u_2 : H_T \to G_T\) which are conjugate to \(u_T : H_T \to G_T\) locally for the étale topology, is precisely representable by the open subset of M, isomorphic to \(G/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(u)\), considered in 5.3.

Let us sketch the variants of the preceding results, obtained by applying 4.1 instead of 4.2. So let G be a group prescheme smooth and affine over S, and denote now by M the S-prescheme, smooth and separated over S, which represents the functor considered in 4.1. We again have actions of G on M: \[ (g, H) \longmapsto \mathrm{int}(g)(H), \] whence as above a morphism \[ (\mathrm{x\ bis}) \qquad G \underset{S}{\times} M \longrightarrow M \underset{S}{\times} M. \]

blocks.label: 5.1 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.1 bis

The preceding morphism is smooth.
Follows from 4.1 and 2.3 bis. One again concludes:

blocks.label: 5.2 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2 bis

Let \(H_1, H_2\) be two subgroups of multiplicative type of G. Then the subfunctor \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(H_1, H_2)\) of G is representable by a closed subprescheme of G, smooth over S.
In particular:

blocks.label: 5.3 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3 bis

Let H be a subgroup of multiplicative type of G. Then the subgroup functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\) of G is representable by a closed subprescheme of G, smooth over S. Moreover, the quotient \(G/\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\) is representable by an open subprescheme of M.

blocks.label: 5.4 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4 bis

Let \(H_1, H_2\) be two subgroups of multiplicative type of G. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(H_1\) and \(H_2\) are conjugate locally for the étale topology.

(i bis) \(H_1\) and \(H_2\) are conjugate locally for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology.

(ii) For every \(s \in S\), denoting by \(\overline{s}\) the spectrum of an algebraic closure of \(\kappa(s)\), the subgroups \(H_{1,\overline{s}}, H_{2,\overline{s}}\) of \(G_{\overline{s}}\) are conjugate by an element of \(G(\kappa(\overline{s}))\).

(ii bis) The structural morphism \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}(H_1, H_2) \to S\) is surjective.

(iii) \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}(H_1, H_2)\) is a principal homogeneous bundle under the action of the smooth S-group prescheme of finite type \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H_1)\).

blocks.label: 5.5 bis
blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.5 bis

Remark 5.5 likewise carries over to the present case.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.6

Note that to establish the result 5.2, and consequently also the first assertion in 5.3, as well as 5.4, the reference to 4.2 can be replaced by a reference to VIII, 6.4, whose proof is much easier. This also shows that the hypothesis G affine over S is unnecessary there. Moreover, in No 6, we shall show how a variant of this method makes it possible to extend these results to the case of certain groups H more general than groups of multiplicative type. These same observations extend to the variants 5.2 bis etc. On the other hand, the result 5.8 below uses in an essential way all the hypotheses made (in particular G affine and smooth over S, H of multiplicative type), and the lecturer knows no proof of it other than via the representability theorems 4.1 or 4.2 7.

As the morphism (x) resp. (x bis) is smooth, hence open, its image is open. Let R be this image, endowed with the structure induced from \(M \times_S M\); one verifies easily that R is an equivalence relation in M, which is moreover none other than the one made explicit in 5.4 resp. 5.4 bis. It would be interesting to know whether the quotient sheaf M/R (which is formally étale over S) is representable (it is then representable by a prescheme étale over S); it is, for example, if S is the spectrum of a field. Note moreover that it may happen that R is not closed in \(M \times_S M\) (even if G is quasi-finite over S…), which means (when M/R is representable) that M/R is then not separated over S.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.8

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-prescheme smooth and affine over S, \(s \in S\). Then:

a) For every subgroup of multiplicative type \(H_0\) of \(G_s\), there exist an étale morphism \(S' \to S\), a point \(s'\) of \(S'\) above s such that the residue extension \(\kappa(s')/\kappa(s)\) is trivial, and a subgroup of multiplicative type \(H'\) of \(G' = G \times_S S'\), such that \(H'_{s'} = H_0 \otimes_{\kappa(s)} \kappa(s')\).

b) For every group homomorphism \(u_0 : H_s \to G_s\), where H is an S-group prescheme of multiplicative type and of finite type, there exist an étale morphism \(S' \to S\), a point \(s'\) of \(S'\) above s such that the residue extension \(\kappa(s')/\kappa(s)\) is trivial, and a group homomorphism \(u' : H' \to G'\), such that \(u'_{s'} : H'_{s'} \to G'_{s'}\) is equal to \(u_0 \otimes_{\kappa(s)} \kappa(s')\).
This follows from 4.1 resp. 4.2, and from Hensel's lemma in the form 1.10.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.9

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-prescheme smooth and affine over S, H a subgroup of multiplicative type of G. Consider \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(H) \hookrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\), which by 5.3 and 5.3 bis are closed subgroup preschemes of G, smooth over S. Then the first group is an open and closed subprescheme of the second, and the quotient sheaf \[ W_G(H) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(H) \] is representable by an open subgroup prescheme of \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr}}(H)\); it is therefore an S-group prescheme quasi-finite, étale and separated over S.

Indeed, consider the obvious homomorphism \[ \theta : \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr}}(H), \] whose kernel is by definition \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(H)\). As \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr}}(H)\) is representable by an S-group prescheme étale and separated over S (X 5.10), its unit section is an open and closed immersion, hence its inverse image under \(\theta\) is an open and closed subgroup of \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\). I claim moreover that \(\theta\) is a smooth morphism: this follows indeed formally from the definitions, and from the fact that \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\) is smooth over S and \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr}}(H)\) is étale over S. One concludes as in 5.3 that the image of \(\theta\) is an open subset U of \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr}}(H)\) and that, endowed with the induced structure, U represents the quotient sheaf \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(H)\). The latter is therefore étale and separated over S since \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr}}(H)\) is, and it is quasi-finite over S, being quasi-compact over S as the image of \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\), which is. This completes the proof of 5.9.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.10

For every \(s \in S\), let \[ w(s) = \mathrm{rank}\ \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{G_s}(H_s)/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{G_s}(H_s) \] (which is also the index of \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{G(k)} H(k)\) in \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{G(k)} H(k)\), where k is an algebraic closure of \(\kappa(s)\)). Then the function \(s \mapsto w(s)\) is lower semi-continuous. For it to be constant in a neighborhood of the point s, it is necessary and sufficient that \(W_G(H)\) be finite over S in a neighborhood of s.

Indeed, for every S-prescheme W which is étale, of finite type and separated over S, it is true that the function \(s \mapsto w(s) = \mathrm{rank}\ [W_s : \kappa(s)]\) is lower semi-continuous, and that it is constant in a neighborhood of the point s if and only if W is finite over S in a neighborhood of s (a fact pointed out in SGA 1, I 10.9, whose proof, which presents no difficulty, will be found in EGA IV 8).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.11

9 Let G be a group prescheme affine and smooth over S, H a subgroup of multiplicative type; then 5.3 and 5.3 bis imply that the quotients \(G/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(H)\) and \(G/\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\) are preschemes smooth over S and quasi-affine over S, since in both cases, the modular prescheme M in which the quotient is embedded is such that every open subset U of M quasi-compact over S is quasi-affine over S (3.11). We shall see moreover in the following exposé 10 that for every U as above, the schematic closure \(\overline{U}\) of U in M is even affine over S, which shows that the quotients under consideration are affine over S provided that the open subset U in the corresponding modular scheme (of group homomorphisms from H to G, resp. of subgroups of multiplicative type of G) is closed in M. This is for example the case if H is a "maximal torus" in the second case considered, or when S is the spectrum of a field, cf. XII.5.4 11. I do not know whether the quotients under consideration are affine over S in general.

footnotes.html: The situation has changed since the writing of this text, cf. XV and XIX n<sup>o</sup> 6.
N.D.E.: For a generalization to the non-affine case, see M. Raynaud, Faisceaux amples sur les schémas en groupes et les espaces homogènes, Lecture Notes Math. 119 (1970), IX.2.8.

N.D.E.: cf. remark XII.5.7. Let us mention here the following generalization. Without any affineness hypothesis on G, if H is a torus and S is locally noetherian, U is affine and smooth by a theorem of Raynaud (loc. cit., IX.2.6 and IX.2.8). Indeed, \(G/\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\) is an open subset of M and the largest representable open subfunctor of M is a disjoint sum of open subsets smooth and affine over S.
N.D.E.: under the hypothesis that G is of locally constant reductive rank.

pages.title: 6. On a rigidity property for homomorphisms of certain group schemes, and the representability of certain transporters12

blocks.html: 6. On a rigidity property for homomorphisms of certain group schemes, and the representability of certain transporters12

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.1

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, H a commutative S-group prescheme of finite type over S, E a set of integers \(> 0\), stable under multiplication, and suppose the following conditions satisfied:

a) For every \(n \in E\), the subgroup \({}_nH = \mathrm{Ker}(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_H)\) is finite and flat over S.

b) For every \(s \in S\), the family of the \({}_nH_s\) \((n \in E)\) is schematically dense in H.

Let Y be a closed subprescheme of H. Then the subfunctor \(\prod_{H/S} Y/H\) of S (compare VIII, 6) is representable by a closed subprescheme T of S, and if S is noetherian, there exists an \(n \in E\) such that \[ (\mathrm{x}) \qquad \prod_{H/S} Y/H = \prod_{{}_nH/S} Y \cap {}_nH/S. \]

Indeed, by virtue of VIII 6.4, as by condition a) \({}_nH\) is finite and flat, and a fortiori "essentially free" over S, it follows that the right-hand side of (x) is representable by a closed subprescheme \(T_n\) of T. Of course, for \(n \in E\), E ordered by divisibility, the \(T_n\) form a decreasing family of closed subpreschemes of S, hence if S is noetherian (which we may suppose) it is stationary for n large. Let T be the value of \(T_n\) for n large; I claim that T does represent the left-hand side of (x), which will complete the proof. We are reduced to proving that if \(S'\) is a prescheme over S such that \((Y \cap {}_nH)_{S'} = ({}_nH)_{S'}\) for every \(n \in E\), i.e. such that \(Y_{S'} \supset {}_nH_{S'}\) for every \(n \in E\), then \(Y_{S'} = H_{S'}\). Now this is indeed the case, for by virtue of IX 4.4 the family of the \({}_nH_{S'}\) is schematically dense in \(H_{S'}\), taking into account conditions a) and b).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.2

Let S be a prescheme, H, E as in 6.1 satisfying conditions a), b), \(u : H \to G\) a homomorphism of S-groups from H to an S-group prescheme locally of finite type over S, and finally K a closed subgroup prescheme of H. Consider the subfunctor \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(u, K)\) of G (cf. 2.5). Then the latter is representable by a closed subprescheme of G, and if G is noetherian (for example S noetherian and G of finite type over S), there exists an integer \(n \in E\) such that \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(u, K) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(u|_{{}_nH}, K)\). If finally G is smooth over S, and K smooth over S or of multiplicative type, and if H, E satisfy the following condition, stronger than a):

a') For every \(n \in E\), the subgroup \({}_nH\) of H is of multiplicative type,

then \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(u, K)\) is smooth over S.

Indeed, consider the G-group \(H_G = H \times_S G\), which evidently satisfies the conditions of 6.1 (S replaced by G, and H by \(H_G\)), and the closed subprescheme Y of \(H_G\), the inverse image of \(K \subset G\) under \[ H_G = H \underset{S}{\times} G \longrightarrow G \] defined by \((h, g) \mapsto \mathrm{int}(g) \cdot u(h)\). Then \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(u, K)\) is none other than \(\prod_{H_G/G} Y/H_G\) (compare VIII, examples 6.5 e)). Hence the first assertions follow from 6.1, and moreover, one sees that for every quasi-compact open subset U of G, there exists \(n \in E\) such that \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(u, K)\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(u|_{{}_nH}, K)\) have the same trace on U. To verify the last assertion of 6.2, one may therefore replace H by an \({}_nH\), and then it suffices to apply 2.5, which applies since \({}_nH\) is supposed of multiplicative type over S.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.3

The preceding proof uses only the very elementary result VIII 6.4, and in addition (for the last part) 2.5, that is, when K is smooth over S, the infinitesimal result IX 3.6, hence the vanishing of the cohomology of groups of multiplicative type. Note that in the most important cases (cf. 6.7) one may even suppose the \(n \in E\) prime to the residue characteristics of S, i.e. invertible in \(\mathscr{O}_S\), hence the \({}_nH\) finite étale over S, and then the cohomological result invoked is practically trivial, so 6.2 is then independent of the theory of groups of multiplicative type.

One sees as usual that 6.1 extends to the case where one has an S-prescheme \(S'\) over S (not necessarily locally noetherian), and a closed subprescheme \(Y'\) of \(H' = H_{S'}\), provided that \(Y' \to H'\) is of finite presentation, i.e. the ideal defining \(Y'\) is of finite type: then \(\prod_{H'/S'} Y'/H'\) is representable by a closed subprescheme \(T'\) of \(S'\), such that \(T' \to S'\) is of finite presentation, and if \(S'\) is quasi-compact, there exists an \(n \in E\) such that the relation analogous to (x) holds. It also follows that the first statement in 6.2 is valid without supposing G locally of finite type over S, provided that the immersion \(K \to G\) is of finite presentation.

As announced in 5.6, theorem 6.2 makes it possible to extend to group preschemes satisfying a') and b) above certain results established by another method and under more restrictive conditions for groups of multiplicative type. This is the case for the results 5.2, the beginning of 5.3, 5.4 and the "bis" variants of the preceding results, and 5.9 and 5.10. The same holds for the results of IX, Nos 5 and 6, with the exception of IX 6.8 (already false for a homomorphism of abelian schemes \(u : H \to G\) over the spectrum S of a discrete valuation ring with residue characteristic \(p > 0\): it may indeed happen that \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u\) has as generic fiber the unit group, and as special fiber a radicial group not reduced to the unit group).

We have just given an example of a rigidity property for groups of multiplicative type which is not shared by abelian schemes. Another example, extremely important, lies in the fact that the existence theorem for infinitesimal extensions of homomorphisms IX 3.6 is no longer valid when H is an abelian scheme. Thus, an abelian scheme \(\neq 0\) over a field admits nontrivial infinitesimal variations, contrary to what happens for a group of multiplicative type — which is the infinitesimal aspect of the fact that there exists a "theory of moduli" (moreover far from being complete) for abelian varieties, whereas the theory of moduli for groups of multiplicative type is empty. Another "global" aspect of this infinitesimal difference is that if H is an abelian scheme over a locally noetherian S, and G a commutative S-group prescheme locally of finite type over S, then one can show that \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr}}(H, G)\) is representable by a prescheme locally of finite type over S, but contrary to what happens for H of multiplicative type, this prescheme is not étale over S, but only unramified over S. Thus, if S is for example the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring, H and G abelian schemes over S, there may exist homomorphisms \(H_0 \to G_0\) on the special fibers which do not come "by specialization" from a homomorphism on the generic fibers.

Theorem 6.2 applies whenever H is an abelian scheme over S, or more generally an extension of such a scheme by a torus. Indeed, the question being local on S, one may suppose that there exists a prime number \(\ell\) prime to the residue characteristics of S, and one sees that it then suffices to take for E the set of powers of \(\ell\) in order to satisfy conditions a') and b).
An argument analogous to that of 6.1 gives us the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.8

Let X be a prescheme smooth over S, with nonempty connected geometric fibers. Then for every closed subprescheme Y of X, the functor \(\prod_{X/S} Y/X\) is representable by a closed subprescheme T of S. If Y is of finite presentation over X, then T is of finite presentation over S.

As \(f : X \to S\) is faithfully flat and locally of finite presentation, it is covering for the fpqc topology. As, on the other hand, \(T = \prod_{X/S} Y/X\) is evidently a subsheaf of S (for the fpqc topology), it follows that the question of the representability of T by a closed subprescheme of S is of local nature on S for the fpqc topology, and the same is true of the question of deciding whether T is of finite presentation over S. After performing the base change \(S' \to S\), with \(S' = X\), we are reduced to the case where X admits a section e over S. We may moreover suppose S affine and a fortiori quasi-compact. We then have:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.9

Under the conditions of 6.8, suppose that S is quasi-compact and that X admits a section e over S. Let, for every integer \(n \geqslant 0\), \(X_n\) be the subprescheme of X, the n-th infinitesimal neighborhood of the section e. Suppose Y of finite presentation over X. Then there exists an integer \(n \geqslant 0\) such that \(\prod_{X/S} Y/X = \prod_{X_n/S} Y_n/X_n\) (where \(Y_n = Y \cap X_n\)).

This corollary does imply 6.8 when Y is of finite presentation over X, thanks to VIII 6.4, for X being smooth over S, \(X_n\) is finite and locally free over S and a fortiori is "essentially free" over S in the sense of VIII 6.1, hence \(\prod_{X_n/S} Y_n/X_n\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of S. Moreover, the proof of loc. cit., or the reduction to the noetherian case, shows us immediately that the said closed subprescheme of S is of finite presentation over S.

Let us first prove 6.9, hence 6.8, when S is noetherian. Let \(T_n = \prod_{X_n/S} Y_n/X_n\); then the \(T_n\) form a decreasing sequence of closed subpreschemes of S, and S being noetherian, this sequence is stationary. Let \(R = \bigcap_{n \geqslant 0} \prod_{X_n/S} Y_n/X_n\) be their common value for n large; we evidently have \(T \subset R\), and it suffices to establish that \(R \subset T\). After performing the base change \(R \to S\), we are reduced to the case where \(R = S\), i.e. \(Y_n = X_n\) for every n, i.e. \(Y \supset X_n\) for every n, and to proving then that \(T = S\), i.e. \(Y = X\). Now \(Y \supset X_n\) for every n implies (thanks to the fact that X is locally noetherian) that Y is, in a neighborhood of each point of \(e(S)\), an induced open subprescheme of X, hence there exists an induced open subset U of X, containing \(e(S)\), such that \(U \subset Y\). By virtue of IX 4.3, the fibers of \(X/S\) being integral, U is schematically dense in X, hence (Y being a closed subprescheme dominating U) we have \(Y = X\). This proves 6.9, hence 6.8, in this case.

The general case proceeds by reduction to the preceding one. For every \(s \in S\), there exist an affine open neighborhood U of s and an affine open neighborhood V of \(e(s)\) such that \(f(V) \subset U\). Then \(f(V)\) is an open neighborhood of s contained in U, and if \(S'\) is an affine open neighborhood of s contained in \(e^{-1}(V) \cap f(V)\), and \(X' = V \cap f^{-1}(S')\), then \(X'\) and \(S'\) are affine open subsets of X resp. S, and \(X'/S'\) admits a section. Because of the local nature of 6.8 and 6.9 we may suppose \(S = S'\). I claim that then \(\prod_{X/S} Y/X = \prod_{X'/S} Y'/X'\), where \(Y' = Y \cap X'\); indeed, by virtue of IX 4.6, \(X'\) is schematically dense in X (at least when X is quasi-separated over S, so that \(X'\) is retrocompact in X; but in fact one can show without difficulty that IX 4.6 remains valid without the retrocompactness hypothesis), and likewise for every base change \(S_1 \to S\), \(X' \times_S S_1\) is schematically dense in \(X \times_S S_1\), whence at once the stated equality. This reduces us to the case where \(X = X'\), so we may suppose S and X affine.

Moreover, if \(X = \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) and if J is the ideal of B which defines Y, J is the inductive limit of its subideals of finite type, hence Y is an intersection of closed subpreschemes \(Y_i\) of X which are of finite presentation over S, and consequently \(\prod_{X/S} Y/X = \bigcap_i \prod_{X/S} Y_i/X\), which reduces us, in order to prove 6.8, to the case where Y is of finite presentation over X. It then suffices to prove 6.9 with S and X affine. But then X and Y over S come by base change \(S \to S_0\) from an analogous situation \(X_0\) and \(Y_0\) over \(S_0\), with \(S_0\) noetherian, which reduces us to the case where S is noetherian, which has already been treated. This completes the proof of 6.8 and 6.9.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.10

Let X be a smooth S-group prescheme of finite presentation, with connected fibers, Y a group prescheme of finite presentation over S, \(i : Y \to X\) a monomorphism of S-group preschemes, thus making Y a subgroup of X. Then \(\prod_{X/S} Y/X\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of finite presentation of S. If S is quasi-compact, denoting for every integer \(n \geqslant 0\) by \(X_n\) the n-th infinitesimal neighborhood of the unit section of X, and setting \(Y_n = X_n \cap Y\), we have for n large enough: \(\prod_{X/S} Y/X = \prod_{X_n/S} Y_n/X_n\).

The proof is essentially that of 6.9. Note that the unit sections of X and of Y induce bijective immersions \(S \to X_n\) and \(S \to Y_n\), hence inducing isomorphisms of \(S_{\mathrm{réd}}\) with \((X_n)_{\mathrm{réd}}\) and \((Y_n)_{\mathrm{réd}}\), which implies that the injection \(Y_n \to X_n\) is proper, hence, being a monomorphism of finite presentation, is a closed immersion. Consequently, by virtue of VIII 6.4 already used, \(\prod_{X_n/S} Y_n/X_n\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of S of finite presentation over S, and it therefore remains to prove the last assertion of 6.10 in the case where we suppose S moreover affine. One again reduces immediately to the case where S is noetherian, and we are reduced to proving that then \(R = T\) (with the notations of the proof of 6.9), or again that \(Y \supset X_n\) for every n implies \(Y = X\). Now the hypothesis implies that \(i : Y \to X\) is étale at the points of the unit section of Y over S, hence Y is smooth over S at the points of the unit section, whence it follows that the open subset \(Y'\) of the points of Y at which Y is smooth over S is an induced open subgroup of Y. Then \(Y' \to X\) is an étale monomorphism by virtue of X 3.5, hence an open immersion; now the fibers of X being connected and every open subgroup of an algebraic group being also closed, it follows that it is a surjective open immersion, i.e. an isomorphism. Hence \(Y' = X\) and a fortiori \(Y = X\), which completes the proof of 6.10.
Proceeding as in VIII 6.5, one concludes from 6.10:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.11

Let G be an S-group prescheme locally of finite type and quasi-separated over S, H a group prescheme smooth of finite presentation over S with connected fibers, \(i : H \to G\) a monomorphism of S-groups, thus making H a subgroup of G. Then:

a) \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(H)\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\) are representable by closed subpreschemes of G of finite presentation over G, and likewise, for every monomorphism \(j : K \to G\) of finite presentation of S-group preschemes, \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(H, K)\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of G of finite presentation over G.

b) If G is quasi-compact, in the various cases considered in a), there exists an integer \(n \geqslant 0\) such that (if \(H_n\) denotes the n-th infinitesimal neighborhood of the unit section of H) we have \[ \begin{aligned} \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(H) &= \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(H_n) \\ \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H) &= \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H_n) \\ \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(H, K) &= \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(H_n, K) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(H_n, K_n). \end{aligned} \]

One applies 6.10 to the group prescheme \(X = H_G = H \times_S G\) over the base prescheme G, and to the subgroup prescheme Y the inverse image of the diagonal subgroup of \((G \times_S G)_G\) over G under a suitable homomorphism of G-groups from X to \((G \times_S G)_G\) (in the case of Centr), resp. the inverse image of \(K_G\) under a suitable homomorphism of G-groups from X to \(G_G\) (in the case of Transp). The case of Norm reduces to the transporter by taking \(K = H\), the hypothesis on G ensuring that \(H \to G\) is of finite presentation (hence \(Y \to X\) is of finite presentation); in the case of Centr, the hypothesis made on G ensures that the diagonal group of \(G \times_S G\) is of finite presentation over \(G \times_S G\), whence again the fact that Y is of finite presentation over X.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.12

One can prove (using rather delicate results from EGA VI 13) that if X is a flat prescheme of finite presentation over S, which is proper over S or has nonempty connected geometric fibers, then for every closed subprescheme Y of X of finite presentation over S, \(\prod_{X/S} Y/X\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of S of finite presentation over S. Likewise, if X is a flat S-group prescheme of finite presentation with connected fibers, and \(i : Y \to X\) a monomorphism of S-groups, with Y an S-group prescheme of finite presentation, then \(\prod_{X/S} Y/X\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of S of finite presentation over S. In particular, 6.11 a) remains valid on replacing the hypothesis "H smooth over S" by "H flat over S".

footnotes.html: The present Number does not use the results of N<sup>os</sup> 3, 4, 5; its natural place would be in VI<sub>B</sub>.
cf. also J.P. Murre, Representation of unramified functors. Applications, Sém. Bourbaki No 294 (Mai 1965), th. 3 (p. 13).
